data.title: ASML outlines €34B–€39B 2026 revenue target amid record Q4, EUV momentum, and major engineering restructuring
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Christophe Fouquet, President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management, highlighted a “record quarter, record year, record booking,” attributing the surge to strengthening AI-driven demand across both advanced logic and memory. Fouquet stated, “The last 3 months have really clarified basically at least the horizon for 2026 and most probably a bit beyond that.” He emphasized that “AI will have also this effect basically to really drive our entire product portfolio in the coming years.”
  • Fouquet noted that 2026 is positioned as a “big year for EUV,” with increased shipments expected. The CEO also discussed the acceleration of lithography intensity, citing that “the number of layers of EUV increasing basically at our key customer,” which signals a greater need for advanced lithography tools.
  • The CEO confirmed long-term targets remain unchanged: “We still expect for 2030 revenue between EUR 44 billion and EUR 60 billion with a gross margin of 56% to 60%.”
  • R.J.M. Dassen, Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board, stated, “Q4 2025, a record quarter by any standard. It was a record quarter in terms of sales. It was a record quarter in terms of order intake. It was a record quarter in terms of cash flow generation.” Dassen specified, “We ended the year with EUR 32.7 billion in net revenue, 52.8% gross margin… net income of EUR 9.6 billion and an EPS earnings per share of close to EUR 25 per ordinary share.”
  • Dassen highlighted that EUV revenue grew 39% year-on-year, driven by a mix of more tools, higher sales prices, and the “recognition of a number of EXE tools, High NA tools.”
  • Fouquet announced a restructuring of the technology organization, resulting in 1,700 job cuts primarily among leadership roles, to “really boost again our engineering capability, our innovation engine.” He explained, “Out of the 3,000 people that we don't need basically to lead the team, we are going to create 1,400 engineering positions.”

Outlook

  • Dassen guided for first quarter 2026 net revenue between EUR 8.2 billion and EUR 8.9 billion, with a gross margin between 51% and 53%.
  • For the full year 2026, net revenue is expected between EUR 34 billion and EUR 39 billion, representing a midpoint growth of 12% over 2025.
  • Dassen stated, “We once again expect the EUV business to go up significantly this year. We also expect the installed base business to go up this year, and it will go a little bit at the detriment of the non-EUV business.”
  • Management anticipates the China business will decrease to around 20% of total sales in 2026.

Financial Results

  • ASML reported EUR 32.7 billion in net revenue for 2025, with a gross margin of 52.8% and net income of EUR 9.6 billion. Earnings per share were close to EUR 25 per ordinary share.
  • EUV was confirmed as the main driver, with 39% growth year-on-year. Leading technologies (EUV and immersion) accounted for 90% of systems revenue.
  • The installed base business reached EUR 8.2 billion in 2025, nearly matching ASML’s total 2017 revenue.
  • Dassen noted a 26% increase in installed base business, attributing the growth to service and upgrades, particularly for EUV tools.
  • Deep UV revenue declined 6%, with most of the decrease attributed to China.

Q&A

  • Sarah Jacob, Bloomberg News, inquired about restructuring costs. Dassen answered, “These costs in the grand scheme of ASML would not be considered material.”
  • Jacob also questioned the sustainability of capacity expansion. Fouquet explained, “We have seen our customer basically moving forward. They start to really believe in the sustainability of the AI demand… and as a result, they started to invest.”
  • Financial Times asked about AI memory shortage. Fouquet responded, “The demand for high bandwidth memory, which is the AI memory is extremely high. But the demand for DDR memory… is also very high. And as a result, we have seen basically the price of DRAM going up significantly in the last few weeks.”
  • An analyst asked about the impact of the stabilizing AI market on job growth in Eindhoven. Fouquet indicated, “The long-term trajectory is still a trajectory of growth… we will be adding jobs in manufacturing and customer service.”
  • Mark [NSA] asked about internal structure changes and supply chain readiness. Fouquet clarified, “The transformation… will be mostly around D&E… everything else is connected to that.” Dassen added, “We have a very solid understanding also with our supply chain. They're doing the same thing.”
  • Chemical Daily inquired about R&D spending split. Dassen stated, “The lion's share of the R&D expense really is an EUV, right?”
  • An analyst queried about the China business decline. Dassen replied, “It is going down in absolute numbers as well… it goes down in system sales, I think, something like EUR 850 million… but it is reduced in comparison to what it was last year. So it's more normalization than that anything very spectacular is going on there.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of AI-driven demand, restructuring impact, and China market normalization, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as most concerns were directly addressed.
  • Management tone was confident in prepared remarks, highlighting “record” achievements and growth potential. In Q&A, responses remained composed, with Fouquet describing the restructuring as “most probably the most difficult decision the management team ever had to make in ASML,” and Dassen repeatedly stating, “We are very well prepared.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, current sentiment is more optimistic on AI-related demand and business visibility, with less emphasis on uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter marked a shift from uncertainty to clarity in business outlook, as Fouquet noted more defined demand for 2026 and beyond, specifically from AI-driven investment.
  • The restructuring of the technology organization was a significant new announcement, compared with last quarter’s management appointments and succession planning.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from visibility and booking linearity to the practical impacts of record demand and organizational changes.
  • Key metrics such as net revenue, EUV growth, and installed base business all rose, while the China sales percentage and Deep UV revenue declined versus last quarter’s projections.
  • Management’s confidence level increased, reflecting stronger order intake and industry tailwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited organizational complexity as a challenge, prompting the decision to cut 1,700 leadership positions to boost engineering productivity.
  • Dassen acknowledged dependency on customer capacity readiness and supply chain coordination to meet rising demand.
  • The normalization of China revenue remains a risk, with Dassen suggesting a continued decrease as the backlog is worked through.
  • Energy cost and availability were flagged as long-term industry risks, more so than precious metal pricing.

Final Takeaway

ASML management underscores that record Q4 results and full-year performance were propelled by surging AI-driven demand, with a robust outlook for 2026 supported by a projected revenue range of EUR 34 billion to EUR 39 billion. The company is executing a significant restructuring to reinforce its engineering and innovation capabilities, while anticipating sustained growth in EUV and installed base businesses. Management maintains confidence in its long-term targets and capacity for continued expansion, despite planned reductions in leadership roles and ongoing normalization in the China market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chipotle brings viral sensation Chicken al Pastor back to its menu
data.description:
Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) plans to bring back Chicken al Pastor as a limited-time protein menu item on February 10 across restaurants in the U.S., Canada, the U.K., France, and Germany. A promotion will see Chipotle Rewards members receiving early digital access on February 9. 

The menu item features marinated chicken seasoned with seared morita peppers, ground achiote, and pineapple juice, finished with lime and cilantro to deliver a bold, tangy, slightly spicy flavor profile. Notably, Chipotle's (CMG) Chicken al Pastor menu option has built a strong fan following since its initial launch as the chain's first global innovation dish. Positioned as the brand’s most-requested limited-time offering, Chicken al Pastor has generated roughly twice as many consumer requests for a comeback as any other Chipotle limited-time offer, due in part to strong social media engagement that can be activated quickly through targeted marketing and loyalty program communication. 

Chipotle (CMG) executives have credited Chicken al Pastor with boosting incremental transactions and contributing to positive traffic trends in the past, suggesting that its return could be a boost for second-quarter earnings results. Analysts noted that premium proteins typically carry higher price points for CMG, meaning the item is likely to support average check growth and comparable sales even if traffic gains are modest.

Chipotle (CMG) last offered Chicken al Pastor in spring 2024 as a limited-time item, following its wider rollouts in 2023 after initial market tests in 2022.

data.title: The Fed should not ignore the inflation target – Fmr. Kansas City Fed president
data.description:
Former Kansas City Fed President Esther George said she firmly opposes the Federal Reserve’s last rate cuts and maintains that the central bank cannot afford to overlook its inflation target.

In an interview with CNBC, George reiterated that she would not have voted for the 25-basis-point cut in December, emphasizing that with inflation running well above target for nearly five years, the Fed must remain vigilant.

“The truth is that the inflation target cannot be ignored,” George stated.

The former Fed official acknowledged that the committee has signaled a pause after implementing three rate cuts, noting their stated concern about supporting the labor market. However, she stressed that the inflation landscape will be shaped by numerous economic movements in the year ahead that warrant close attention.

George said she would be watching how the Fed judges the risk—whether it sees a “two-sided risk” that justifies a wait-and-see approach.

Sentiment surveys from both consumers and businesses are sending clear signals about economic anxiety. “High prices are a problem. People don’t like high prices,” she said, adding that the combination of elevated prices and limited hiring activity has soured public sentiment even as the broader economy appears to be moving forward.

When asked about proposals to cut rates to improve consumer affordability through cheaper car loans and mortgages, George cautioned against such intervention. “There is a high cost of good intentions as well,” she warned, explaining that the cost would come in the form of creating more demand when inflation is already elevated.

George argued that stimulating additional demand through rate cuts would ultimately become “an attack against the purchasing power of the very consumer that you are trying to help.”

Rather than heavy intervention, she advocated for allowing market dynamics to play out naturally.

The long-term remedy, according to George, requires calibrating interest rate policy to bring supply and demand into balance. Once equilibrium is achieved, the economy can grow without consumers constantly worrying about prices—a concern that was largely absent for more than two decades but has now become a dominant factor in how Americans view their purchasing power and standard of living.

More on interest rates

  • S&P 500 tops 7,000 level on Fed day, big tech earnings amid Iran 'massive armada' turmoil
  • U.S. economy gone from slowdown to worries about overacceleration – Apollo Global’s Torsten Slok
  • Orrstown outlines 5% loan growth target for 2026 while expanding fee income and capital investments
  • Fed rate decision preview: Markets expect a hold, but who dissents?

data.title: Top performing healthcare stocks in the past month
data.description:
As the first month of 2026 comes to an end this week and earnings season accelerates, below is a list of the top 10 healthcare stocks with market capitalizations of $10B or more, ranked by their one-month price performance percentage.

The list includes companies operating across various healthcare industries, such as Biotechnology, Life Sciences Tools and Services, Health Care Equipment, Health Care Services, and Pharmaceuticals.

The list is topped by Moderna (MRNA), which showed an impressive 45.67% one-month performance. Revolution Medicines (RVMD) and Revvity (RVTY) follow, with strong performance percentages of 23.62% and 18.46%, respectively.

Among the top 10 performers, two stocks carry particularly strong quantitative recommendations. Gilead Sciences (GILD) and Guardant Health (GH) both currently hold a Quant Rating of Strong Buy, with scores of 4.61 and 4.88, respectively.

Here is the list:

  1. Moderna (MRNA), 1 month performance percentage: 45.67%

  2. Revolution Medicines (RVMD), 1 month performance percentage: 23.62%

  3. Revvity (RVTY), 1 month performance percentage: 18.46%

  4. Bio-Techne (TECH), 1 month performance percentage: 15.21%

  5. Penumbra (PEN), 1 month performance percentage: 13.39%

  6. Gilead Sciences (GILD), 1 month performance percentage: 13.05%

  7. Illumina (ILMN), 1 month performance percentage: 12.70%

  8. Guardant Health (GH), 1 month performance percentage: 11.27%

  9. DexCom (DXCM), 1 month performance percentage: 9.03%

  10. Elanco Animal Health (ELAN), 1 month performance percentage: 8.94%

Health Care ETFs: (XLV), (VHT), (IHI), (IXJ), (IYH), (FHLC), and (FXH).

data.title: Johnson & Johnson upgraded at Morgan Stanley on growth outlook
data.description:
Morgan Stanley upgraded Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) to Overweight from Equal-Weight on Wednesday, noting that the New Brunswick, New Jersey-based pharma giant sits in a “higher growth” group relative to its peers.

Analyst Terence Flynn projects the company’s revenue and earnings per share to record a ~5.5% and 12% CAGR over 2026-2030, respectively, indicating a favorable growth outlook compared to its biopharma rivals and positioning JNJ “in the ‘higher growth’ cohort.”

The analyst raises his estimates for key J&J (JNJ) products Tremfya, Icotyde, Tecvayli, and Darzalex, and argues that JNJ’s patent cliff is “in the middle of the group, but the company's new product cycle offering nearly fills the hole.”

Flynn raised his price target on the stock to $262 from $200 per share, noting that J&J (JNJ) is trading at a ~3x discount to the S&P 500 (SP500). The benchmark index trailed the company by nearly 30 percentage points last year.

The analyst opined that further expansion to JNJ’s multiple is unlikely but added, ”We do see a path to EPS-driven beats from one of the most robust new product cycle offerings in Biopharma, where in aggregate we are ~20% above consensus.”

data.title: Visa, Mastercard quarterly earnings seen trending up on payment volume growth
data.description:
The two biggest U.S. card payment networks — Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) — are set to release on Thursday their earnings for the last quarter of the calendar year, providing further data on how well consumer spending is holding up.

From the data already released tracking holiday spending, which accounts for about two-thirds of 2025's last quarter, the news is good. The National Retail Federation calculates that holiday sales rose 4.1% Y/Y for the period spanning Nov. 1 to Dec. 31, 2025. Adobe's data shows online holiday spending rose 6.8% Y/Y for the same period. Visa's (V) preliminary data for a seven-week period starting Nov. 1 saw an overall 4.2% Y/Y increase across all payment types, while online spending rose 7.8%.

Mastercard (MA) will report Q4 earnings Thursday before the market opens. It's expected to post adjusted EPS of $4.24, according to the average analyst estimate, compared with $4.38 in Q3 and $3.82 in Q4 2024. Revenue is expected to rise to $8.78B from $8.60B in Q3 and $7.49B in the year-ago quarter.

Total gross dollar volume is projected to grow 9.7% Y/Y to $2.81T, according to the Visible Alpha consensus. Payment network revenue is expected to climb 13% to $4.98B, according to the Visible Alpha estimate.

Visa (V) will release its fiscal Q1 earnings on Thursday after the market closes. It's expected to post adjusted EPS of $3.14 for the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2025, up from $2.98 in Q4 FY2025 and $2.75 in Q1 2025. The consensus estimate for Q1 revenue is $10.7B, vs. $10.7B in the prior quarter and $9.51B in the year-ago quarter.

Visa Inc. payment volume is expected to rise 8.7% Y/Y to $3.83T, according to the Visible Alpha consensus, with processed transactions up 9.3% to 69.7B.

In December, Bank of America Securities upgraded Visa (V) to Buy from Neutral, pointing out that the company is one of six in the S&P 500 that produces over 10% revenue growth, double-digit EPS growth, and an operating margin exceeding 50% in the past year.

The number of swipe fees that the companies get from traffic on their networks likely won't be the only point of focus. During Mastercard and Visa's earnings calls, analysts are likely to ask about regulatory pressures the companies are facing with the introduction of the Credit Card Competition Act that's intended to control swipe fees. Both companies' stocks also dipped after President Donald Trump proposed a 10% interest rate cap on credit cards for a year.

Both regulatory actions could have unintended negative consequences on consumer spending, said Citi analyst Bryan Keane, who has Buy ratings on both Visa and Mastercard.

For his expectations on the two networks, Keane sees CY4Q25 payment volumes "trending relatively consistent with the prior quarter (in-line with expectations and cross-border volumes & VAS [value-added services] revenues expected to remain supportive of growth targets)."

On guidance, Citi expects "Visa to reiterate its FY26 guidance, and MA to set low double digits to high-end of low double digits non-GAAP adjusted constant currency Y/Y revenue and operating expense growth for FY26."

Treading Softly, investing group leader of The Dividend Kings, sees Mastercard's (MA) 28.4 forward 12-month P/E ratio as warranted due to its “vigorous” growth profile.  "If Mastercard lives up to growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could have a 13% total return potential in 2026. By the end of 2030, Mastercard could generate nearly 16% annual total returns," the investing group leader said. Mastercard's (MA) risks, though, include increasing regulatory pressures and the potential for a major cyber breach, according to the article.

SA Analyst Perseus Perspectives keeps a Buy rating on Visa (V), despite the regulatory headwinds from the proposed interest rate cap and the competition bill, noting that the company's exposure to interest rate caps is limited. "Its revenue is mainly from transaction processing, not direct lending," the analyst wrote. Regarding the rate cap, the likely outcome is a compromise between industry and the White House, "which will give up a little bit in order for a political victory to be scored." Perseus Perspectives' confidence in Visa's business model remains strong.

Despite the drama over the regulatory threats, Evercore ISI analyst Adam Frisch sees a low probability that the measures will be implemented. That, along with Visa's (V) and Mastercard's (MA) solid underlying fundamentals, creates "a particularly attractive risk/reward setup heading into earnings (similar to volatility caused by the Durbin Act in 2010)," he wrote in a note to clients. Frisch has In Line ratings for both companies.

data.title: Southwest Airlines Q4 preview: What to expect
data.description:
Southwest Airlines (LUV) is set to announce fourth-quarter earnings on Thursday, and investors will watch out for demand trends and updates from the airline about its capacity as well as strategic changes, including  international partnerships, fleet monetization, and rationalized CapEx.

Wall Street expects the Dallas-headquartered company to post an EPS of $0.57, implying a 1.8% increase year-over-year, while revenue is expected to rise 8.8% to $7.51B for the quarter.

The airline’s CEO Robert Jordan, during the Q3 earnings call, said he expects to deliver more than $1B of incremental EBIT from assigned extra legroom seating in 2026 and hit a full run rate of approximately $1.5B in 2027.

Earlier this month, JPMorgan upgraded Southwest Airlines to Overweight from Underweight, with a price target of $60.

JPMorgan analyst Jamie Baker said that the airline’s core business is starting to stabilize and believes its initiatives (bag fees and seat assignments) are progressing as planned, as evidenced by management reiterating incremental EBIT targets throughout 2025, despite the demand headwinds in the industry.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system also considers it a Buy.

The company’s stock has gained over 31% in the last one year, compared to a 16% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

However, Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha analysts remain cautious, rating the stock a Hold.

“Management expects modest capacity growth, incremental EBIT from assigned seating, and potential fuel price relief in 2026. However, Southwest Airlines remains expensive despite anticipated profitability improvements and upcoming Q4 results,” highlighted Seeking Alpha analyst Gytis Zizys, rating it a Hold.

Over the last two years, LUV has beaten EPS estimates 75% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 63% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen two upward revisions versus 12 downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen two upward revisions, compared with nine downward moves.

data.title: SK hynix secures 70% of Nvidia's HBM orders for Vera Rubin: report
data.description:
The South Korean memory maker SK hynix (HXSC.F) has secured about 70% of Nvidia's (NVDA) high-bandwidth memory orders for the Vera Rubin, its latest computing platform for artificial intelligence and high-performance computing applications, according to Yonhap News Agency.

The 70% allocation to SK hynix for HBM4 is more than the earlier market estimate of 50%, the report said, citing sources familiar with the situation. SK hynix is expected to account for 54% of the global HBM4 market in 2026, followed by Samsung (SSNLF) at 28% and Micron (MU) at 18%. Nvidia and AMD (AMD) have also completed quality testing with Samsung's HBM4 products.

The news emerged on the same day SK hynix reported record revenue and operating profit for the fourth quarter of 2025 as the rapid development of AI infrastructure boosted demand for memory chips.

SK hynix also announced it will establish an AI solutions firm, tentatively called AI Company (AI Co.), in the U.S. to find new AI growth engines.

Earlier this week, it was reported that SK hynix will be the exclusive supplier of advanced memory for Microsoft's (MSFT) new AI chip, the Maia 200.

data.title: UBS can handle Swiss capital clampdown as draft law takes shape - report
data.description:
Switzerland is pressing ahead with plans to tighten capital rules on UBS Group (UBS), judging the impact to be manageable as political backing for tougher, post-Credit Suisse crisis safeguards outweighs concerns about competitiveness, according to a Wednesday media report.

UBS (UBS) shares slid 2.7% in late morning trading in the U.S., paring its year-to-date gain to +3.5%.

The Swiss finance ministry's plan would require UBS (UBS) to fully capitalize its foreign subsidiaries through its Swiss unit, a move expected to increase the bank's capital needs by about $23B. But that has received pushback and would compare unfavorably with a lawmaker-backed plan to use AT1 bonds instead of full CET1 capital to back foreign units.

After UBS' (UBS) emergency takeover of former rival Credit Suisse in 2023, authorities are drafting legislation this year to go before parliament in 2027, with provision of full capital backing for UBS' (UBS) overseas operations at the center of the reforms, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar. It would take seven years for UBS to implement the estimated $23B headline rise in capital requirements.

The Swiss government is also signaling flexibility on another element of its reforms targeting so-called intangible capital, including deferred tax assets and software, Bloomberg reported. Such measures were estimated to raise capital requirements by roughly $3B if the wider package of foreign units moves ahead, and they can be implemented without going to parliament. 

Swiss Finance Minister Karin Keller-Sutter last week came out against the AT1 compromise plan, arguing it fails to address the core risks. Meanwhile, the Swiss People's Party (SVP), Switzerland's largest political party, has backed the plan allowing UBS (UBS) to use AT1 to meet part of the new capital rules.

UBS (UBS) didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.
